mnemonics.title: F-Distribution Mean
mnemonics.content: MEAN = Denominator divided by (Denominator minus 2). THINK: DF2 OVER DF2-MINUS-2 (works only when DF2 > 2)
mnemonics.usage: Remember the formula for mean of F-distribution quickly during exams

mnemonics.title: F in ANOVA
mnemonics.content: F IS BOSSY - F = MSB/MSW (Mean Square Between divided by Mean Square Within)
mnemonics.usage: Recall which mean square goes where in ANOVA calculation

mnemonics.title: F-Test Decision
mnemonics.content: F-RIGHT REJECTS - Right-tailed F test: Reject if calculated F > critical F value

mnemonics.title: Variance Existence
mnemonics.content: VARIANCE NEEDS 4 - Variance exists only when denominator df > 4
mnemonics.usage: Remember the condition for variance existence

acronyms.usage: Uses F-distribution to compare means across multiple groups
Variability between group means in ANOVA
Variability within each group in ANOVA; also called MSE (Mean Square Error)
Total, Between, and Within sum of squares in ANOVA calculations

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a long tail extending to the right, starting from 0. The peak is on the left side. As df increases, the peak moves right and shape becomes symmetric.
Think of F-statistic as ratio comparing two variances. When variances are equal, F ≈ 1. When numerator variance is larger, F > 1.
Small df1,df2 = highly skewed, right tail long. Large df1,df2 = bell-shaped, centered around 1. Draw multiple curves showing this transition.
Visualize a pie: Total SS = SS Between + SS Within. F compares how much of total variation is explained (Between) vs unexplained (Within).

analogies.analogy: Think of F-statistic like comparing two thermometers. If both read same temperature, ratio is 1. If one thermometer fluctuates more, ratio deviates from 1.
Like a diversity comparison in a class. If students in different groups vary a lot between groups but little within groups, we get high F (diversity between matters).
Imagine measuring height in different cities. F-test asks: Are height differences BETWEEN cities greater than height variations WITHIN each city? If between > within, cities are truly different.
Think of df as sample size information. More df = more information = distribution becomes more predictable (normal-like). Less df = less information = more uncertainty, more skew.
